aliases: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. rubra

english: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. rubra

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw red cabbage is approximately 89 g (1 cup shredded), commonly used as a salad base, coleslaw ingredient, or garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Cabbage, red, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Cabbage, red, raw (cr_id F14010013). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat value cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169975 (Cabbage, red, raw) where HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; red cabbage estimated from cruciferous vegetable GI data; University of Sydney GI Database entry for cabbage (raw)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for red cabbage raw exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 16 is estimated from the closest approved analogous entry: raw cabbage (white/green, GI ~10–20, University of Sydney GI Database). Red cabbage has a similar carbohydrate profile (7.37 g/100g) and fibre content (2.1 g/100g). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (16 × 6.56) / 100 ≈ 1.1, where carbohydrate per 89 g serving = 7.37 × 0.89 = 6.56 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (27 mg/100g) and high potassium (243 mg/100g) yielding a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio that supports blood pressure regulation.
hypertension.guidance: Raw red cabbage is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals; its high potassium-to-sodium ratio (9:1) actively supports vascular tone, and it should be consumed without high-sodium dressings or pickled preparations to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~16) and low carbohydrate content (7.37 g/100g) with 2.1 g/100g dietary fibre that moderates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw red cabbage is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (~16), low carbohydrate load, and 2.1 g/100g dietary fibre; it can be freely included in diabetic meal plans provided sugary dressings are avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (2.1 g/100g), and anthocyanin polyphenols with documented cardioprotective antioxidant effects.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw red cabbage supports cardiovascular health through its near-zero saturated fat and cholesterol content, high fibre, and anthocyanin-rich pigment that has been associated with reduced LDL oxidation and improved endothelial function in peer-reviewed literature.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane from cruciferous glucosinolates and anthocyanin polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory properties; vitamin C at 57 mg/100g supports collagen synthesis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw red cabbage is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its sulforaphane and anthocyanin content; patients on warfarin should monitor vitamin K intake from cruciferous vegetables and consult their prescriber.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw red cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. rubra) is a cruciferous leafy vegetable characterised by its deep purple-red leaves, crisp crunchy texture, firm core, and mildly peppery flavour. It is consumed raw in salads, coleslaws, and as a garnish, or lightly cooked by steaming, stir-frying, or braising. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 31 kcal, 1.43 g protein, 0.16 g fat, 7.37 g carbohydrate, 2.1 g dietary fibre, and 27 mg sodium. It is exceptionally rich in vitamin C (57 mg/100g), potassium (243 mg/100g), and anthocyanin polyphenols responsible for its vivid colour and antioxidant activity. Its very low glycemic index (~16) and low carbohydrate density make it ideal for diabetic and weight-management meal plans. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio (9:1) supports blood pressure regulation, making it suitable for hypertensive individuals. Sulforaphane from glucosinolate hydrolysis provides anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease prevention. Raw preparation maximises sulforaphane, anthocyanin, and vitamin C retention. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, with negligible allergen risk.

red cabbage: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. rubra

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14010013) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169975 for red cabbage raw. Vitamin C value (57 mg/100g) consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (raw cabbage entry) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.02 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.08 g/100g), trans fat (0.0 g/100g), and detailed micronutrients (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169975 (Cabbage, red, raw) as HPB Foodo does not report these fields. Values are consistent with the HPB macronutrient profile. 2. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per both HPB and USDA; red cabbage is a plant food that naturally contains no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100g and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; plant foods do not synthesise vitamin B12 or vitamin D3. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; no industrial trans fats are present in raw vegetables. 3. GI of 16 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database raw cabbage entry; no direct GI measurement for red cabbage exists in the approved GI database. GL per 89 g serving = 1.1. 4. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are defensible matches for red cabbage. SNOMED CT code 226734001 is cited as a defensible match; reviewers should verify against the current SNOMED CT release. 5. This is a generic raw ingredient record; no portion data was present in the input. A standard 89 g (1 cup shredded) serving has been assigned based on common culinary convention. 6. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this generic Western ingredient; no Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory language rules apply.
